data.title: FMC Corporation: High-Risk Play In Fertilizers That I'm Unwilling To Enter
data.description:
Readers have contacted me over the past few months, wanting me to start coverage on the company FMC Corporation (FMC). I've never been opposed to it, but my coverage on fertilizers and agrochemicals has been at capacity for some time - though this is at least partially an excuse. The simple fact is, that I've never been a fan of the fundamental weakness that I've been able to see in FMC, even before the crash that we saw in 3Q of 2025. The recent pieces of news have not made this any easier, not in the least.

You know me for a fundamental valuation investor, if you follow my work. The importance is in those two concepts. Fundamentals, one, meaning I want strong fundamentals or at least something I can understand. Secondly, valuation. Once I understand it, I want a good price for the relative risk that I am taking. The reason I have been so reticent on FMC is because I believe that the company has either failed to deliver one of those things or both.

This article represents my first entry (and possibly last; we'll see what happens) into coverage for the company according to my valuation model, but don't mistake that for a high conviction rating. In fact, I rate it a "SELL". In this case, I believe that Seeking Alpha's valuation and Quant grades very nicely encapsulate what the picture of this company is. Quant has it at 11/12 (in spots in the industry), and 260 out of 280 in the sector. It's not a good ranking. Literally every single factor grade apart from valuation is at a D+ or worse - and the only reason that valuation is at A+ is because you're getting the companies for what in the long term could amount to "pennies on the dollar".

Many analysts have been positive on the company, especially since the drop. They see the value in the assets, and the potential for an upside that certainly is here.

But given what we're seeing in the fertilizer/agrochemical sector, is it worth it? Is it a risk worth taking here, given what the company said about its 2026E?

Let's see what we've got here.

FMC - Issues were clear 3+ years ago

The reason I have stayed out for so long was that FMC presented some of the most unappealing fundamentals in the sector - and it did not take the 2025 results or the downturn to bring that. Warning signs, to any analyst who was looking, were clear. In early 2023, the company started seeing destocking across multiple key channels. While this was in no way unique at the time, the degree, coupled with the company's market exposures to markets like Brazil, which wasn't as crucial for Yara (YARIY) or others, was worrying. Anyone who looked at top-line sales declines could see more than a 25% decline in a single year, and the fact that it was so much more than management had guided for, was one of the first signs.

The reason I like Yara, and even Bayer (if we want to compare agrochemicals more purely), is its conservative financials and fundamentals. Low debt, high cash flow, and capital mean that when, not if, a downturn hits, Yara is capable of fending this off with its reserves for quite some time before things get "bad".

That's not, dear readers, the case with FMC. In 2023, the company had a debt load of over $4B net. When you combine this with its 2024E FCF of negative $800+ or so, you could see very early on what the danger for this company was. If you're a company burning through cash during a downcycle, things can get bad - real bad. Case in point, FMC was forced to lower its prices despite low margins at the time, while others were stabilizing or even raising them.

Very little of what FMC has gone through the past 2-3 years has been good news or positive trends. The poor performance in India was just "icing on the risk cake" here. When FMC reported revenues that had not yet been sold to final customers, I was already not interested in the company, but this seemed to be the final straw to many institutional investors, who then left the investment. And it's not though, after 3-4 successive guidance cuts between 2023-2025, that investors were not warned.

Staying in an investment because you believe in a turnaround, while fundamentals stay solid, is one thing. Staying in it while fundamentals and earnings significantly worsen, which means the valuation decline has correlation, is another thing - at least to me.

So, FMC. The company is an agroscience company working in both fertilizers and various types of plant chemicals. It's not as "pureplay" as, say, Yara, and focuses more on protection, agrotech, and the like. Overall, not a bad company nor a bad sector to be in - complementary to pure-play fertilizers (FMC produces fertilizers such as Crop Up and NPK 6-23-1, as well as various plant stimulators), even. I've invested plenty in Bayer and made good profits, and I've invested plenty in Yara, same good profits (and more there). In the end, both fertilizer and agrochemical companies focus on improving crop yields.

Many of its products are sold in NA, APAC, and LATAM. Brazil, in particular, is a major revenue source for FMC due to high-yield corn and soybean production, and FMC has focused large sales efforts on it. Through the M&A of Cheminova, the company is growing in Europe/EMEA as well.

India was once a good market for the company, but unfortunately, the company is selling the segment, valued at about $450M, to reduce debt. As of 4Q25, the company's net debt to EBITDA was about 4.1x, which for this sort of company is very high. As a comparison, Yara is at 1.17x, Bayer is below 3x.

FMC reported its 4Q25 results not long ago, according to the timing of this article. It's a good coincidence, because my stance is that even in the event of the company basically hitting all of its targets and priorities, which you see above, the company would still be fundamentally questionable. The debt paydown won't bring its net debt down below 3x- and FMC is still forecasting lower prices as I am writing this article for 1Q26. Volumes may be up, but pricing is still dropping, and India remains a headwind (5% for 1Q26E), despite FX tailwinds reported.

Combining lower pricing and unfavorable manufacturing costs, the picture is not good. Furthermore, about $20M in tariff headwinds. The company's outlook for adjusted EPS is still negative here, with an estimated AEPS of negative $0.44/share.

Whenever you're looking to invest in a company with negative AEPS that has had fundamental troubles for some time, one of the first questions to ask is, "When will things turn around"? The recent dividend cut does not make it easier, and the fact that the "strategic review" is ongoing further complicates matters. It has gotten so bad that FMC is considering selling off business units.

How exactly would that work? Is it realistic, or even likely? It's obvious that $2.2B+ in net losses requires some sort of management action. So retaining services from legal and financial advisors is entirely understandable. However, the company's view that the stock price doesn't reflect its asset base - well, the debt is part of the fundamentals, thanks to company action and management action. This was further emphasized by the rating cut from Moody's in early 2026 to junk status, or Ba1.

Their mere statement that they are open to selling it means that there are PE firms currently in rooms, calculating what would be possible to "extract" from this company following a sale.

6 months ago, positive AEPS called for $5.02 in 2028E with a high yield, about $4.65 in 2027, and about $4/share in 2026E. Now the forecast is less than half that - and that's on an adjusted basis.

The thing is, PE firms have consumed FMC "parts" before. In 2013, Peroxygens was sold to a PE firm at about $200M, and Cinven bought Global Specialty Solutions in 2024 for about $350M. Therefore, the risk of the company buying the entire company is, in my view, relatively low. Rather, I would "pick and choose" the chocolate from the cookie here - it's unlikely that all of the company is attractive for purchase.

This also leads me to how I consider the company in terms of valuation.

Valuation

This is one of those rare times when I'm not giving the company a clear valuation. My experience with LBOs or PE firms is limited, but it's there.

So let me be clear on this - in most PE/LBO (leveraged buyouts), common shareholders do not keep their shares. They typically get an amount over the price before the announcement. The real danger is if not all parts of the company are sold. This would initiate a carve-out process, where sales proceeds are unlikely to land in your pockets (unless you sell on announcement), and you're instead stuck in a multi-year turnaround, which would then be dependent on what hypothetical parts of the company remain and are attractive.

A PE is not going to buy unattractive assets at appealing prices - they're likely to want the highest-margin, high-potential businesses. To get even close to $1-2B out of a deal like that, based on previous PE deals for FMC, this company would likely have to sell off part of its "identity" and core.

The notion that a buyer is going to price your assets at a price that "you want", or at a price that's appealing to you, during a fire sale/debt restructuring, is very naive. If you have FMC shares, and you've held them for long, it's my stance that you're unlikely to recoup your investment (I may be wrong, of course).

A PE firm would likely value the company depending on the debt paydown plan. But given the company's very volatile earnings, the potential analysis here is very dangerous to begin with. Existing debt makes even PE deals sensitive to interest rates and operational underperformance. Because of the market backdrop for fertilizers, which I've gone into in my ICL (ICL) article, I don't see this as likely to outperform in 2026.

Instead of putting a clear valuation from the get-go, let me pose a question back to you.

At what price would you buy a distressed, uncertain EBITDA, highly leveraged company with market issues in a volatile market that's experiencing top- and bottom-line weaknesses?

Because that needs to be the question. As a buyer, you wouldn't listen to arguments about how the company "may" be nice in the future. The 5-year EBITDA average is about 15x. If I were a PE company, I wouldn't go in above 3-5x here - but again, this would depend on a lot of moving parts. The current book value is at 0.87x. You might think this is low, but it's actually not that low. When you consider how low the company has fallen, you'd expect it to be maybe 5x lower than its peers, but it's not even half of what Bayer is trading at.

If FMC wants to sell off Crop Protection, then we need to see key segment data for that segment. If it's agroscience and R&D, then we need to see that (maybe higher multiples). The problem is that the company provides regional, not clear sector, results or forecasts. This makes valuing it harder, because the company isn't (likely) going to sell more geographies (though we'd have to see).

The high use of debt, the distressed state of the business, and the considerations that a PE company is likely to do mean that I don't view the company as investable at this point, except in the most extreme speculative scenario. If you want a good upside from that hypothetical deal price (and I stress: hypothetical), you'd need to buy at significantly below what the company would be sold, or sell its parts for - you'd also (if the company isn't sold in its entirety) need to be willing to hold for a long time, likely at sub-par returns.

To account for that, I would be unwilling to buy above 3.5x EBITDA, and above 0.4x book value. A low-end 3.5x $720M EBITDA level implies a $2.5B EV for the entire company - about $20/share based on shares outstanding. But then we also have the debt, which puts the share price technically negative. I realize this is also well below the company's targets for sales proceeds, but that's how I value it. That's the issue here. Bullish investors are citing macroeconomic and demographic trends in support of their stance - I believe we're way past that here.

I just don't believe there's a whole lot of value here. The company's legacy products are facing generic pressure, the diamides are losing margins, and the growth focus and R&D is an uncertain segment at best. Current SOTP valuations range from between $17-$38/share. Analyst targets for the company are now at a low of $13/share to a high of $40/share, with an average of $18/share - but despite the upside, only 4 out of 17 investors are at "BUY".

I would buy shares in FMC at a single-digit share price. Below $10/share, so a PT of $9.9. And even then, I would be very careful. This means reducing a significant debt impact from the EV/EBITDA, and hoping for a great deal of upside from a PE sale. It's below the lowest estimates. I use it because I don't see a recovery for its legacy or its diamide segments in the current market, and I see the downgrade in ratings further pressuring the company's financials and interest rate payments (which have been growing more than 60% in less than 4 years).

This is a very bearish thesis, but it's one I can "stand behind".

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

I believe this company is in deep distress and very hard to profitably invest in. Its current state as an admitted PE target (potentially selling the business), means that it's extremely difficult to see where we are in a year, or even 6 months. What's clear is that it's not a good time to be a long-term FMC shareholder if you bought at a high price.

The only way I would invest in this company would be at an extreme discount to valuation and normalized EBITDA - I'm talking pennies on the dollar, accounting for their debt. I'm valuing the company at deep EBITDA discounts, mirroring typical PE logic at 3-5x EBITDA, coming to an implied share price of $9.9/share - and even then, I doubt this one would be the first on my list with everything available on the market today.

This is the first company I review that only fulfills one of my criteria - cheapness. I also rate the company a "SELL" here, because I believe the uncertainty from what is going on is too high.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 0 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $9.9/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "SELL" rating as of February 2025.

data.title: Franklin Templeton ClearBridge Small Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fourth quarter capped a volatile and demanding year for small cap investors. The Russell 2000 Index returned 2.2% for the quarter and finished the year up 12.8%, trailing the Russell 1000 Index by approximately 450 basis points. A partial rotation away from the dominant AI trade supported value stocks over growth during the quarter, with the Russell 2000 Value Index returning 3.3% compared to a 1.2% gain for the growth index.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), Celcuity (CELC) and Photronics (PLAB). Stock selection in the financials and energy sectors and underweight to IT.
  • Detractors: Holdings in Concept Therapeutics (CORT), MP Materials (MP) and Penguin Solutions (PENG). Stock selection in the materials, IT and communication services.
  • Outlook: Despite near-term headwinds, attractive relative valuations and early signs of earnings broadening point to a more constructive setup for patient investors.

Performance Review

  • The Fund generated positive absolute performance but trailed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, as notable strength in financials and energy, along with contributions from select health care holdings, were offset by weakness in materials and consumer discretionary.
  • Beneath the surface, market leadership remained uneven. The quarter began with a continuation of one of the most extreme low-quality rallies on record, as non-earners, low-return on equity companies and high-beta stocks dramatically outperformed, leaving active managers broadly behind. This dynamic briefly gave way to a more constructive environment, as leadership rotated toward value, quality and lower-beta stocks, allowing small caps and active strategies to outperform for a short period. That improvement ultimately proved fleeting, as momentum leadership reasserted itself into year-end, closing the quarter with narrow breadth and elevated speculative activity.
  • Financials strength reflected strong stock selection within consumer finance and specialty credit. OneMain Holdings (OMF), a provider of personal loans and related financial services, benefited from resilient credit trends and improving visibility into earnings as macro conditions stabilized. Encore Capital (ECPG), which focuses on debt recovery and receivables management, also contributed positively as investor sentiment improved toward companies with demonstrated cash flow generation and disciplined balance sheet management in a benign credit environment.
  • Energy was another area of relative strength, driven by company-specific execution and favorable industry fundamentals. Weatherford International (WFRD), an oilfield services provider and a new position, advanced as improved operational performance and sustained upstream activity supported earnings momentum. International Seaways (INSW), a global tanker operator, benefited from constructive tanker market conditions, including favorable freight rates and tighter vessel supply. Green Plains (GPRK), a biofuels and agricultural processing company, contributed as margins improved and investor focus increased on the company's strategic repositioning and longer-term earnings potential.
  • Health care delivered mixed results as a sector but accounted for a large collection of the Strategy's top individual contributors. Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals and Celcuity led performance on continued progress across their respective pipelines, while Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM), Tarsus Pharmaceuticals (TARS) and Avidity Biosciences (RNA) added further positive contributions tied to clinical and regulatory developments.
  • Health care gains were offset by weakness in Concept Therapeutics and Acadia Healthcare (ACHC). Concept declined following the issuance of a Food and Drug Administration Complete Response Letter for its relacorilant program, which introduced uncertainty around the timing and commercial potential of a key pipeline asset. Acadia Healthcare, meanwhile, detracted after lowering its outlook to reflect higher anticipated costs and increased legal-related reserves, which pressured near-term earnings expectations.
  • On the detractor side, materials weighed on relative results due to stock selection. MP Materials, a producer of rare earth materials, declined as sentiment toward certain commodity-exposed names softened following strong prior performance and shifting expectations around demand. Eagle Materials (EXP), a supplier of construction materials, also detracted amid increased sensitivity to cyclical construction trends and input cost dynamics.
  • Consumer discretionary was another area of relative weakness. Stride (LRN), an education services provider, declined as concerns emerged around enrollment trends and near-term growth visibility, while Visteon (VC), an automotive electronics supplier, detracted amid volatility tied to global auto production and demand expectations.
  • We also adjusted our exposure to consumer sectors with the addition of staples company Interparfums (IPAR) and Signet Jewelers (SIG) in discretionary. Interparfums develops and markets prestige fragrances under long-term licensing agreements with global fashion and luxury brands, and we believe the market is undervaluing the company's long-term growth potential as it broadens its portfolio across brands and fragrances. Meanwhile, Signet Jewelers, the largest specialty jewelry retailer in North America, has shown signs of stabilization following a multiyear post-COVID demand contraction. We see improving execution, strong cash generation and ongoing capital returns supporting the company's recovery as demand normalizes.
  • We also exited Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX), a Hawaii-focused real estate company, following the announcement of its agreement to be taken private, and Beta Bionics (BBNX), a medical device company focused on automated insulin delivery systems, as the investment case became increasingly dependent on longer-dated outcomes, leading us to reallocate capital toward opportunities with clearer near-term visibility.

Outlook

  • We remain realistic about the challenges facing active small cap investors in the near term. The market's preference for speculative growth, leverage and momentum has persisted longer than historical precedent would suggest, and periods of narrow leadership can continue to test patience. In such environments, outcomes can be unforgiving, and dispersion can remain elevated.
  • That said, starting conditions matter, and we believe the current setup is increasingly constructive. Small cap valuations remain compelling relative to large caps, earnings leadership has the potential to broaden, and early, albeit intermittent, rotations toward quality and value suggest the market may be becoming incrementally more responsive to fundamentals. While we do not attempt to time inflection points, history suggests that periods of extreme factor dominance are often followed by more balanced leadership.
  • As enthusiasm for low-quality extremes fades and fundamentals reassert themselves, we believe disciplined, bottom-up portfolios focused on durable business models, strong balance sheets and long-term value creation are well-positioned to benefit. While the path forward may not be linear, we are confident that patience, selectivity and adherence to our investment discipline will be rewarded as market conditions normalize.

data.title: Brookfield Corporation: Still One Of My Favorite Compounders For The Next Decade
data.description:
There is a chart that is a little troubling about the market right now. It is the forward P/E of the S&P 500 (SPY) and the returns for the next 10 years, and this chart shows that there is a certain correlation between the two. And after 20x, historically, the return for the next 10 years is much closer to low single digits per year. This is not an absolute truth, as it depends on several factors, such as the actual growth of companies in the future, the composition of the S&P 500 at the time, and so on. But in any case, it is enough to say that when expectations are high and indexes are expensive, returns are generally compressed.

And that, for me, goes hand in hand with the case for Brookfield Corporation (BN). I don't think the market right now is an obvious buy, and yet, if we think about other trends such as the devaluation of the USD, geopolitical risks, and inflationary risks, even having a lot of cash in American Treasury doesn't seem so obvious and safe.

But Brookfield still seems to me to be something close to a “safe haven,” a case closely linked to real (and quality) assets, good prospects that allow for compounding, and a very reasonable valuation, and Q4 comes as another point to illustrate this. Therefore, little by little, I continue to increase my position in BN stock.

Brookfield's Q4: Another Solid Quarter

The market reaction to Q4 earnings was practically neutral. The stock rose slightly but stayed at +1% and +2%. And I think that's a totally accurate reaction. That was more or less my interpretation after reading the releases. It was a good earnings report, with some negative and some positive points, but it's not “big news” that would cause the stock to rise 10% or fall 10%.

But the point I see as positive is that the results and the entire positioning of management only reinforce that this is not a one-quarter job. The focus is not only on the small things that happened but rather on how this will position Brookfield to maintain high compounding over the next 5, 10, or 15 years.

Breaking down the results further, Asset Management (BAM) had another very solid quarter, with fee-bearing capital ending the year at $603 billion, and fundraising was very solid throughout the year, with $24 billion coming from retail and wealth clients, showing how the company continues to have very synergistic areas.

Wealth Solutions also had a very solid year and quarter, with annuity sales reaching $20 billion and insurance assets of $143 billion, not to mention the success of initiatives such as the institutional funding agreement, which contributed $2 billion. Further demonstrating how well Brookfield's areas work together, in the Wealth Solutions portfolio, the company managed to invest $13 billion in Brookfield-managed strategies, with a yield of ~8.5%.

For operating businesses, we also had some very solid points, such as BEP and BIP maintaining very solid results, which are the renewable power and infrastructure businesses. This was even clearer in the letter, which included a section highlighting that the goal is “to Partner With the Best,” where Brookfield detailed agreements with NVIDIA (NVDA) to invest in AI factories and AI infrastructure in general, with plans to invest $100 billion. In addition, there was an agreement with Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) with very long-term contracts (~20 years) to sell hydroelectric power, amounting to a few billion. And also a deal to sell (at least) $80 billion in nuclear reactors (a partnership between US Government, Brookfield, and Cameco (CCJ)); all of these deals occurred throughout 2025.

But this was not exactly reflected in Q4. If you look at the table below, even though distributable earnings (DE), which is Brookfield's main metric, were good in 2025, growing just over 10% YoY, note that in Q4, there were virtually no gains, with the company going from $1.498 billion to $1.499 billion.

Now looking at it line by line, you can see that in the quarter, in terms of DE metrics, BAM did very well, Wealth Solutions did well, and BIP and BEP, as mentioned, maintained solid momentum, but BPG contributed negatively. BPG is the company's real estate arm, and real estate is not an industry that is experiencing very interesting momentum, but the result was not as bad as it seems.

Part of this is related to portfolio optimization, as Brookfield sold $24 billion of real estate, so even though it invested more than that, it will take some time for it to mature and turn into DE again.

Of course, this is not exactly positive news, since even with portfolio optimization, considering both the quarter and the year, it is clear that real estate is not a division that is strong right now. And this may even challenge Brookfield's “secular compounding” thesis a little, since YoY DE did not rise but fell and is lower than historic levels.

This may also explain the market's more neutral reaction, since we did not see such positive DE growth or totally bullish expectations, and there are still uncertainties about how these investments in infrastructure/real estate will unfold in the coming years, not least because this also depends on macroeconomic factors.

The good point is that when reading the letter and/or listening to the earnings call, you notice a certain optimism about real estate. Brookfield mentioned that rents are “20% higher than expectations,” and this also has to do with a resumption of quality demand for its properties. For example, some very important tenants, such as Visa (V) and JPMorgan (JPM), are relocating to Brookfield properties. It also takes some time for this transition to occur completely (changing tenants, renovations, recognizing it in the income statement, etc.).

So, when we look only at real estate, it seems like it was a very bad quarter, but it is more of a quarter and a year of transition. In addition, the expectation is that as interest rates fall globally and liquidity improves, these real estate and infrastructure businesses will be able to perform better than we saw in 2025. In fact, in 2026 there are several positive points that management is optimistic about, one of which is strong international expansion, including the U.K. and Japan, with billions and billions to capture in this industry:

“Over GBP 50 billion of pensions are expected to come to the U.K. risk transfer market in 2026 and over GBP 500 billion of pensions will come to the market over the next decade. This represents a large and growing opportunity set. [...]

[...]At the same time, we are working to grow our footprint in Asia where savings products continue to be in high demand as populations age and retirement income is highly desirable. Japan's life and savings insurance market is one of the largest globally with approximately $3 trillion of assets on insurer balance sheets today, reflecting the depth of long-term savings and retirement liabilities that create significant opportunities for retirement income and growth-oriented solutions in the region. [...]”

As I mentioned, DE is not at its historical peak, but this is easily explained by other factors, such as portfolio optimization, realized gains, carried interest, and so on, and I don't think it detracts from Brookfield's merits, especially considering BAM's strength in Q4. In short, as I mentioned earlier, I don't think it was a big surprise, but this quarter shows an alignment with long-term deals, quality assets, and all of this turning into healthy distributable earnings in the coming years.

Risks for Brookfield Corporation

There are some risks in Brookfield's case, and we have seen some of them this year. In fact, I believe that many are also implicit when you ask yourself whether it is worth investing in BN or investing only in BAM. Personally, I invest in BN because, although I recognize that BAM is really the crown jewel, the consolidated business is also very good. Brookfield, for example, can directly ride the compounding of real assets, while BAM does so more indirectly through fees. Other than that, Brookfield also has other important points for growth in the coming years, such as Wealth Solutions, which is also something that BAM captures more indirectly.

But the risk points are that while Brookfield captures these upside movements directly, with “real” exposure to assets, often owning them (and in return also having more debt), it also has the greater risk. As I mentioned in the section above, 2025 was not a strong year for real estate, and as much as I believe that the properties are truly of quality and that we are already seeing a certain level of transition, there is this risk of execution and portfolio.

If there is a disruption in office buildings, BN stock suffers dramatically because NAV decreases significantly, and in addition, the real estate business is still important in the composition of DEs.

Another risk for Brookfield Corporation that I see is precisely that of execution. I am convinced that these deals that are happening now, and all the value that Brookfield is investing and the billions it plans to invest in 2026, will yield high returns and compound the DE.

However, execution risks and external risks do exist. Much was mentioned in the last letter about AI. On the positive side, I believe that having exposure to AI via Brookfield is one of the “safest” ways, because as Brookfield itself mentioned, it is not trying to predict which LLM will be the winner but rather is focusing on infrastructure:

“Our focus is not on predicting which models will win, which chips will dominate, or which applications will ultimately prove enduring. Instead, we are focused on the structural need for large-scale, long-duration infrastructure capacity to support the growth of ai, and on investing in the essential physical assets required to enable it.”

But we are still talking about a sector that is very sensitive, with absurd prices and numerous competitors, and all this investment, if this “secular trend” does not materialize as expected, may end up yielding a lower return.

Bottom Line: Brookfield is Still Great

These uncertainties will always exist in a business like this, with intensive investments, real assets, and a diversified portfolio, so I still think Brookfield remains a great opportunity for compounding over the coming decades. The year 2025 showed this well; even though real estate did not do so well, the strength of BEP, BIP, and BAM, and the growth of Wealth Solutions compensated for this and maintained high growth.

Other than that, valuation remains a central pillar of the case, with 2025 DE per share ending at $2.54, or a price-to-DE of ~18x. So if, over the next 10 years, the company manages to achieve its goal of growing this indicator by, say, 15% (and in a more optimistic scenario, it could reach the high teens), DE could reach more than $10, implying a price-to-DE of 4.6x in 2035. So even though BN stock has recovered from the dip I mentioned in my last article (return of ~8%), I still think it's a good enough valuation.

In other words, I believe this multiple is attractive mainly because of the quality and good prospects we have. I don't think BN is a case that will suffer a high re-rating or impress with growth much higher than the high teens this year, so I also don't think it's a stock that offers asymmetry, such as a 100% upside in a few quarters. It is a case of compounding, where every year the DE increases a little, the multiple changes little, and the stock rises a little, but in the long run it makes a big difference.

The chart below shows what I mean. Even though in years like 2022 the price-to-DE was much more compressed than today (something closer to ~10x), in 2021 the multiple was already closer to ~15x, so even though today there seems to be a certain “premium” in the P/DE compared to some periods, it still seems interesting to me because, on average, EBITDA, cash flow, and other financial metrics advance close to the stock price, compounding over time.

As I do not expect Brookfield to suffer a high de-rating, I believe this should be reflected in the stock price, and therefore, I maintain my strong buy rating on Brookfield.

data.title: Sonic Automotive Deserves To Move Higher
data.description:
Historically, I have had a pretty good track record in the market. Over the last year, for instance, two of my three portfolios are performing exceptionally well. My largest one is up 55.3%. My smallest is up 20%. Am I a medium-sized one, which is focused on padding me in the event that I am correct about the economy entering into a recession, is up 3.72%. All combined, we are looking at a return of about 25.8% in the last 12 months. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up only 14.8%. The NASDAQ is up about 17.9%. Unfortunately, not all of my calls are always right, though. One prospect that has so far disagreed with me is Sonic Automotive (SAH), a rather interesting player in the automotive retail space. Since I called the company a ‘buy’ back in August of last year, the stock has dropped 13.6%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 9.3%.

This drop in share price has been in spite of the fact that revenue has increased nicely. And honestly, it seems to be largely because of mixed financial performance on the bottom line. Certain profitability metrics are doing well, while others could be better. I do suspect that, based on broader economic concerns, the picture for the business will worsen in the near term. But because of how cheap the stock is, I remain optimistic enough about it to maintain it as a ‘buy’ candidate.

Soon, we will get some idea as to whether I was right or not. I say this because, on February 18th, before the market opens, the management team at the company will be announcing financial results covering the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. The good news is that revenue is expected to rise. But the bad news is that profitability is forecasted to fall slightly. While this would be disappointing to see, unless the drop in profits is significantly greater than what analysts anticipate, I don't see myself changing my assessment of the company at that time.

Shares remain compelling despite challenges

Before we get into expectations for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, I think it would be helpful to touch on the most recent financial performance provided by management. Involves the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to the company, revenue came in at $3.97 billion. This was actually a pretty solid increase over the $3.49 billion the company reported a year earlier. The company benefited from strong demand in the automotive market. US retail new vehicle sales, for instance, jumped from 12.9 million on an adjusted annual basis to 13.6 million year over year. And when we consider that the fleet new vehicle demand remained flat at 2.7 million, we get a total new vehicle jump from 15.6 million to 16.3 million.

This might seem peculiar considering my own view about the economy. However, this actually makes a lot of sense when you consider what went on last year. For starters, car buyers decided to front-load their purchases in anticipation of higher costs associated with tariffs. Estimates vary. However, according to Cox Automotive, in an article published in August of last year, the average increase caused by tariffs should be around $2,800 per imported unit. For some vehicles, the amount is naturally higher. But even vehicles produced in the US will be hit by tariffs. I say that because while about half of all new vehicles sold in the US are produced here, somewhere around half of the content that goes in them is produced overseas. Another catalyst for front loading purchases involved the electric vehicle tax credit that expired at the end of September. That was a $7,500 tax credit. And clearly, buyers of electric vehicles frontloaded their purchases in anticipation of that expiration.

During the quarter, Sonic Automotive generated retail new vehicle revenue of $1.87 billion. That was well above the $1.57 billion reported a year earlier. A good chunk of this increase came from higher prices per new retail unit. On a per-vehicle basis, the increase was from $54,676 to $58,368. But in addition to this, there was a surge in the number of units sold from 28,657 to 32,086. Used vehicle sales for the company improved also. Revenue jumped from $1.18 billion to $1.25 billion. Some of this was because of a rise in the number of units sold from 43,474 to 44,167. But the bigger driver was a rise in the revenue per unit from $27,158 to $28,372.

Although revenue for the company improved year over year, profitability took a hit. Net income actually contracted from $74.2 million to $46.8 million. Unfortunately, the big pain point for the company came from new vehicle retail sales. Even though the price per unit increased and overall volumes rose, gross profit per new retail unit contracted from $3,056 to $3,035. For new fleet units, the drop was even sharper, from $1,596 to $23. Unfortunately, the company had to contend with greater competition. This was competition based on pricing. So even though it did push higher costs onto its customers, because of this competitive environment, it was not able to push enough of those costs to offset the higher expenses that it incurred.

Other profitability metrics for the business ended up being mixed. Operating cash flow, for instance, surged from $87.8 million to $167.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $110.4 million to $89 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business grew from $135 million to $149.4 million. The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Once again, revenue for the company improved. But profits and cash flows were inconsistent. The biggest issue ended up being net profits, which contracted sharply. But the good news was that all of the other profitability metrics improved year over year.

Management has expressed some optimism moving forward. For instance, the company continues to focus on acquisition activities. In August of last year, for instance, the company acquired some additional assets, namely Jaguar Land Rover Santa Monica, in an effort to expand its operations through the Southern California market. That increased the number of Jaguar Land Rover dealerships that the company has in the US to 11. This makes it the largest such retail ownership group in the US based on volume. The firm is continuing to push for growth associated with its EchoPark business, and management mentioned in their third-quarter earnings call that they expect margins to improve in the final quarter.

If we project out results for the company based on what we have seen already for 2020 live, we would get net profits of $98.5 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $414.2 million, and EBITDA of $635.5 million. Using these estimates, and the results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced. Admittedly, the stock is quite pricey relative to earnings. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to the five companies that I compared it to in that chart. But when it comes to the other valuation approaches, the stock is actually quite cheap.

Soon, we will get to see exactly how the company performed during the quarter. At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year on February 18th. That should occur before the market opens. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate revenue of $3.94 billion. That would be an increase over the $3.90 billion the company reported a year earlier. But because of continued margin pressures, which management said would be alleviated to some extent in the quarter, earnings per share should drop from $1.61 to $1.56. That would mean a decline in net income from $58.6 million to $54.8 million. In the table below, you can see how the firm performed in the final quarter of 2024 when it came to revenue, profits, and cash flows. It is highly likely that a decline in net profits will result in cash flows declining as well. But that is something for investors to keep a close eye on.

The last thing I will say is that I found the overall inventory situation of the company to be surprising. Given the significant buying that occurred late last year for the reasons I mentioned earlier, I would have expected inventories to have been at least partially depleted. But actually, when it came to its franchise dealership operations, new vehicle inventories dropped only a bit, from 57 days to 51 days. Used vehicle inventories remained flat at 34 days. Meanwhile, for the EchoPark business, there was an increase from 33 days to 37 days. One thing I will say is that, since the expectation is that the number of new vehicles sold in the US is negative for this year, with a decline from 16.3 million vehicles last year to 15.8 million this year, this drop in inventories on the new unit side could be a positive for the company. Having less inventory gives it more flexibility to keep prices elevated, even during times of weakness.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain optimistic about Sonic Automotive. I suspect that when management announces results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, we will see that they anticipate some industry weakness this year that should negatively affect organic results. However, the stock is incredibly cheap relative to cash flows. And even though the expectation is for additional weakening to occur, that's not enough to offset this discount in my view.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Copper Coiling, Profit Growth Booming, Raising My Target
data.description:
Copper prices remain firm after a torrid 2025 rally. US prices are near $6 per pound, and that has been incredibly favorable for Freeport-McMoRan (FCX). The world’s largest copper producer has seen its earnings soar, along with its share price. After posting strong Q4 results last month, I see more upside ahead.

I had a buy rating on FCX in December. The stock has been on an outright heater, up 46% since my previous assessment. Today, I reiterate a buy rating and am lifting my price target from $55 to $69.

FCX Outperforming SPY & XLB

Copper Prices Near $6/lb

Back in January, Freeport posted a strong set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.47 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.32, while revenue of $5.6 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $280 million beat. Its average realized price of copper was $5.33 and $4078 for gold. The company plans to phase in its Grasberg block cave restart in Q2, so that’s a possible upside catalyst.

Despite the solid numbers, FCX dipped 2.9% in the session after reporting, actually its worst post-earnings performance going back to April of 2023. That didn’t do too much to cool the bulls’ sentiment, however. Ahead of the April 23 earnings event, the options market prices in a somewhat high 6.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the event. Implied volatility remains high at 50.7% on the $90 billion market cap stock. Short interest is low at 2.33%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, it was an all-around impressive operational performance. Copper and gold sales topped the firm’s expectations for Q4, and unit net cash costs verified more favorably than predicted. Specifically, copper sales reached 709 million pounds (compared to an estimated 635 million), and gold sales reached 80 thousand ounces (60,000 estimated).

2025 Summary

Freeport tallied $2.0 billion in adjusted EBITDA, which was sharply above forecasts. Higher gold and copper prices were the obvious tailwind, but credit to the company for keeping costs in check—the unit costs actually fell in the period. The Materials sector stalwart continued progress on Grasberg remediation.

2026 Outlook

So, why did the stock dip? The 2026 outlook was conservative. For 2026, FCX slightly reduced copper and molybdenum sales guidance...though it did raise gold output estimates, keeping Grasberg’s longer-term outlook intact. Unit cash costs were guided at $1.75 per pound, which is better than its industry peers.

As for capex, the management team lifted that forecast by 5% to $4.3 billion. Despite no buybacks and a modest Q4 free cash outflow tied to working capital, liquidity and profitability measures appear very sound.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong again this year after the profit trough that played out from 2023 through much of last year. By fiscal 2028, Freeport could near $4 in operating per-share earnings. Estimates are already up significantly since my late-2025 analysis.

Indeed, the sellside has turned quite bullish, as evidenced by a high 14 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days compared to just a lone downgrade. Finally, profitability trends are best in class, earning the stock a pristine A+ Factor Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system.

FCX: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my December figures. If we assume $2.90 of forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS and apply a 24x multiple, then shares should trade near $70. I increased the EPS assumption based on prevailing consensus numbers while taking up the multiple due to the higher out-year profit expectations.

Even a 24x P/E suggests a low PEG ratio of under 1 based on FY 2027 bottom-line growth.

FCX: Strong EPS Growth Yields a Low PEG Ratio

Key risks for Freeport include lower copper and gold prices, which could certainly come about, given the extreme volatility ongoing in the industrial and precious metals spaces. Slower global economic growth would be a more fundamental headwind, while uncertainty on the tariff front is still a concern.

Of course, the threat of natural or operational disasters is high with a labor- and asset-intensive company like this global miner. The Grasberg mine is clearly in focus among street analysts—any hiccups in production there could weigh on the stock price. Higher capex may also lead to higher unit costs, pressuring margins and the bottom line.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Thursday, April 23, BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still inexpensive on valuation, FCX’s technical situation looks good. Notice in the chart below that the stock is above a long-term area of polarity. Last time, I offered color on a symmetrical triangle pattern. Well, the bulls quickly took shares out of that consolidation phase, and FCX soared through the previous all-time high range between $52 and $55. And that high congestion area I was concerned about in Q4? Well, former resistance becomes new support.

So, the mid-$50s should be a long-term buying spot—I don’t expect FCX to fall below it any time soon. Still, after the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph reached technical overbought conditions, shares have dipped. Long-term, take a look at the 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. For now, $55 is support, while $69-$70 is resistance.

FCX: Bullish Long-Term Breakout Targets $80+

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on FCX. The Q4 beat was encouraging after a wild (though net bullish 2025). I raised my price target, and the chart shows a long-term breakout.

data.title: Comstock Resources: Cost Progress In The Western Haynesville
data.description:
Comstock Resources (CRK) presented during the conference call solid proposals to decrease Western Haynesville costs. This should have been expected. But it may have taken the market somewhat by surprise. Additionally, the company added three rigs and completion crews because natural gas prices have rallied quite a bit from the previous fiscal year. All of this is going to change the overall numbers in terms of adding reserves. Keep in mind that the Western Haynesville acreage is still being evaluated. That evaluation process overwhelmed the earnings report in a big way. The company has 4 rigs in the Western Haynesville. Therefore, there is likely a solid profit opportunity that is not yet apparent to the market.

During the conference call, management was confident that the natural gas costs for the Western Haynesville would continue to decline. This is a normal situation for much of the current upstream business. Management discovers a potential find and then takes a few years to optimize the development plan. We really will not know the final costs for developing the acreage for some time.

Last Article

The last article noted that the company raised cash by selling noncore areas. Then the company got lucky because winter temperatures have aided natural gas prices to the point where the company posted a fourth quarter that was not predicted during the fiscal year until recently. The first quarter might be even better depending upon how the winter finishes.

Now the midstream company that is helpful for the Western Haynesville apparently will go public in yet another strategy to remove the preferred stock while decreasing the necessary profit for the midstream profit to be adequate. Preferred stock can be expensive capital. A successful going public attempt could therefore materially lower overall costs.

The biggest change from the last article is the various strategies that should result in tangible cost reductions throughout 2026. This is likely to change some of the breakeven calculations out there. The difference between management goals and public breakeven costs (calculated goals) is a goal to continue to decrease well and completion costs to a certain level.

This is something I dealt with in accounting. We regularly budgeted cost decreases without really having a clue as to how to get there doing the budget. But we were assured that there were places to look and things to try. That was combined with reasonable assurances that "something" was (likely) going to work (because it worked before). Many times, management thought they knew that "something" ahead of time. But a process like this always had a few surprises.

But the summary has to be that cost reduction has to continue. Management definitely has goals on this. But the unknown is the challenges along the way to meet those goals. Sometimes things go faster than expected. Sometimes there are delays. But usually, management does get where it is going. However, patience is required.

Western Haynesville Costs

In the meantime, the costs are still high compared to anyone I follow.

Interestingly, this took up a significant number of analyst questions. Analysts are in the position of being forward looking. But management, for obvious reasons, is not disclosing the ultimate goal (mainly because they might not get there or they might go past it). Continuing technology improvements make the cost reduction process easier as the company proceeds to lower costs.

The interesting part was that this was an earnings call with almost no interest from the analysts in the earnings.

However, the constant evaluation of more acreage could very well be distorting progress. Keep in mind that cost reductions benefit a portion of the geology, not all of it. But that will become a general statement over time. What is shown above is an average of all kinds of activity.

There was a question about why some wells cost more. Basically, the answer was that a different process was needed for those wells. This factors into well profitability.

Whenever there is a slide in a well's payback or profitability, it needs to be remembered that slide usually represents an average or a management goal for the current fiscal year. Actual acreage results vary a surprising amount. The only companies I follow that report on this regularly are Peyto (PEYUF) which regularly publishes the profitability results of all wells, and Advantage Energy (AAVVF) which publishes the payback time periods for their wells.

The Western Haynesville is likely more profitable than is apparent due to the constantly decreasing costs, the higher initial production, and the on-average shorter lateral lengths. What we do not know from the presentation is the decline rate of the wells drilled (really anywhere). That is a key part of the profitability picture.

But one can assume that if management has directed 4 rigs to the Western Haynesville, it is because they see a solid profit opportunity. There is absolutely no reason to assume an illogical conclusion (of a very high breakeven) that I have seen pop up in various places.

Even with expensive wells with high-cost production, if they are drilled in the right environment, then everything after payback is profit. Dropping the costs simply means there are more "right environments".

Summary

The current earnings report really was overwhelmed by the excitement surrounding the Western Haynesville discovery and acreage evaluation. There were questions about the finances and the amount left on the bank line (which has calculated liquidity of currently over $1 billion available). But most of the interest is directed at the resources available to the Western Haynesville and the progress made.

There is a very good possibility that the Western Haynesville will prove to be a lower cost resource than the legacy acreage. But it will take a few years to be sure of that. Keep in mind that the evaluations going on in the Western Haynesville are at least somewhat sensitive to the commodity price environment. What is going on there is at the very least partially optional activities. Any company, including this one, will head towards "sure things" in a hostile commodity price environment. That could change a lot of figures that we are now seeing significantly. It may well account for the lack of interest in current earnings.

In line with the market interest, management has a significant part of the budget set aside for Western Haynesville activities. The immediate earnings impact would likely be if Pinnacle Gas Services goes public in fiscal year 2026. That could well provide the company with a one-time gain in the same sense that the acreage sale did for the fiscal year just ended.

My suspicion is that with all these activities and goals, the Western Haynesville is already profitable in the current environment (high costs and all). But now management wants to get more competitive costs for the future because they are reasonably sure that they can.

Comstock Resources remains a strong buy idea. The Western Haynesville still has the ability to remake the company's profitability in the future. My guess is that it will be more profitable than the legacy acreage. But that covers a wide range of possibilities. It is the best I can do right now.

Depending upon "final results", this stock is likely to be a good deal higher than it is now.

Risks

The Western Haynesville can disappoint in whole or in part at any time in the future. Past successes do not guarantee future successes.

Similarly, technology advances could stop at any time while cost reduction efforts could begin to fail.

Upstream is a whole lot more predictable than it used to be when I was a kid. But there is still a fair amount of upstream risk. Well results often will vary considerably even when they are near each other.

The company likely acquired the Western Haynesville acreage cheaply (and on good terms). Therefore, if part of the acreage disappoints, that may not affect the ultimate management goals. In fact, some percentage of disappointing acreage might be part of management's plans already.

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies - January 2026 Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price) or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalisation-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalisation-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), OR Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%; therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were recalculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025, March 2025, April 2025, May 2025, June 2025, July 2025, August 2025., September 2025, October 2025, November 2025, December 2025.

As of the end of January, the biggest precious metals R&S company was Wheaton Precious Metals, with a market capitalisation of $59.9 billion. Wheaton was followed by Franco-Nevada ($45.2 billion) and Royal Gold ($22.2 billion). At the bottom of the ranking was Star Royalties (STRFF), with a market capitalisation of only $23.6 billion. The biggest change that occurred in the ranking in January is the addition of Summit Royalties (SUMMF), which, with a market capitalisation of $84 million, sits at the third position from the bottom.

In January, all the companies except for Metalla (MTA) (-3.47%) and Vizsla Royalties (VROYF) (-5.92%) recorded share price gains. Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) even recorded extreme returns as high as 157%. The company benefited from its high exposure to silver, whose price exploded in January. Vizsla Royalties benefited from its silver exposure too; however, a late January security incident at the Panuco project erased all the gains.

In January, gold and silver prices soared to new historic record highs, at $5,608/toz and $121/toz, respectively. This resulted in the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) growing by 12.27% and the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) growing by 17.11%. The precious metals miners did well too, and the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) grew by 9.83% and 9.06%, respectively. But the precious metals R&S companies did even better. The Precious Metals R&S Index grew by 13.44%, and the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index by 20.92%.

The January News

The majority of January news was focused on preliminary Q4 attributable production and revenue announcements. However, also several small deals took place.

Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) was recognised as one of the 2026 Global 100 Most Sustainable Corporations, just like Franco-Nevada.

Franco-Nevada (FNV) declared its 19th consecutive dividend increase. This time, the dividend will grow by approximately 16%, to $0.44 per quarter. The next payment will be made on March 26 to shareholders of record as of March 12. The company was also recognised as one of the 2026 Global 100 Most Sustainable Corporations. The company will release its Q4 financial results on March 10, after the market close.

On January 26, Franco-Nevada announced a $100 million purchase of a gold stream from Orezone (ORZCF). According to the terms of the stream, Franco-Nevada will be entitled to fixed deliveries of 1,625 toz gold per quarter over the first 5 years and 5% of gold produced at the Casa Berardi mine thereafter. The ongoing payments will amount to 20% of the spot gold price. Orezone will use the proceeds to finance the acquisition of the Casa Berardi mine and other Quebec assets from Hecla Mining (HL).

Royal Gold (RGLD) announced preliminary Q4 sales of approximately 64,000 toz of gold equivalent. This number also includes contributions of Sandstorm's assets, following the October 20th completion of the acquisition. The company also reduced its debt to $825 million as of January 12 and announced some minor non-core asset rationalisation. Royal Gold will report its Q4 financial results on February 18, after the market close.

OR Royalties (OR) announced preliminary Q4 deliveries of 21,735 toz of gold equivalent. The total 2025 deliveries amounted to 80,775 toz, which means that the guidance of 80,000-88,000 was met. The preliminary Q4 revenues grew to a record high of $90.5 million. As of December 31, OR Royalties held cash of $142.1 million and no debt. The company will release its Q4 financial results on February 18, after the market close.

On January 29, OR Royalties announced the acquisition of an additional 1% NSR royalty on the Namdini gold mine in Ghana, growing the royalty interest to 2%. OR agreed to pay up to $103.5 million for the 1% royalty. The mine is operated by Shandong Gold (SDGMF) and should be producing around 287,000 toz gold per year on average over a 15-year mine life.

Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM) reported preliminary Q4 sales of 28,757 toz of gold equivalents and preliminary Q4 revenues of $118.9 million. The overall 2025 numbers amount to 113,237 toz and $388.7 million, respectively. The company will release its Q4 financial results on February 18, after the market close.

Elemental Royalty (ELE) optioned three exploration-stage assets in the Serbian Bor mining district to BHP Group (BHP). BHP can acquire the properties by expending $5 million on exploration of the properties, making a $200,000 payment to Elemental on the 6-month anniversary of the agreement, and making annual payments of $200,000 until the earn-in is completed. Elemental will retain a 2% NSR royalty.

On January 9, the company announced that it granted 663,339 options, 155,133 RSUs, and 14,919 cash-settled DSUs to some of its officers, directors, employees, and consultants.

Versamet Royalties (VRMTF) announced a C$125 million bought deal financing and concurrent private placement. The plan is to issue 9.1 million shares at a price of C$13.75 per share. The net proceeds should be used to reduce the outstanding debt and to fund potential new acquisitions.

Gold Royalty (GROY) reported preliminary Q4 revenues, land agreement proceeds, and interests of $5.2 million. The total 2025 number amounts to $17.7 million. Gold Royalty will release its Q4 financial results on March 18, after the market close.

On January 14, the company announced the acquisition of an additional 1.5% NSR royalty on Aura Minerals' (AUGO) Borborema project. Gold Royalty agreed to pay $22.5 million for this asset.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) transformed its 35% gold offtake agreement over the Santa Luz, Fazenda, and RDM mines into a 29% production-linked offtake stream of 29% of refined gold produced at the Greenstone mine, with minimum deliveries of 63,600 toz in 2026.

Vizsla Royalties noted a security incident that occurred at Vizsla Silver's (VZLA) Panuco property. Ten individuals were taken during the incident, and Vizsla Silver subsequently suspended certain activities in the area. Vizsla Royalties holds a 2-3.5% NSR royalty over the Panuco project.

Orogen Royalties (OGNNF) announced the 2025 highlights and 2026 outlook. In 2026, 12 partner-funded drilling programs consisting of around 40,000 meters (excluding Ermitano drilling) are expected. Moreover, new resource updates are expected at Navidad/Winter and Ermitano.

Nations Royalty (NRYCF) announced that Derrick Pattenden will replace Robert McLeod as President and CEO. The company also made a C$15 million private placement under which it issued 9.375 million shares and 9.375 million share purchase warrants with a strike price of C$2.25 and a maturity of January 30, 2029.

Empress Royalty (EMPYF) granted 250,000 restricted share units to Daniel Burns, following his appointment to the Board of Directors.

Summit Royalties (SUMMF) started trading on the OTCQB Venture Market as of January 16. The company also provided a portfolio update. The most important news is the expected cash flow growth in 2026, fueled mainly by the Madsen mine ramp-up, Zancudo mine expansion, and Bombore mine expansion.

Silver Crown Royalties announced that Michael Gentile made a C$3 million strategic investment in the company. The investor acquired 424,500 shares and 424,500 share purchase warrants with a C$8.25 strike price and three-year maturity. Following the financing, Michael Gentile's securities represented 18.52% of issued and outstanding shares on a partially diluted basis.

The February Outlook

After the gold and silver prices reached their record highs in late January, they experienced steep declines. Since then, the prices have been very volatile. The same applies to the share price of precious metal miners and also precious metals R&S companies. As a result, it is very hard to predict what the remainder of February is going to bring.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adobe Looks Interesting At 7-Year Low (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My last Adobe Inc. (ADBE) article was a Sell suggestion in October 2023 here. For sure, avoiding the stock would have helped readers to sidestep a -48% price loss since then. Even worse news is the S&P 500 has gained +66% in price over the last 29 months. Investing elsewhere would have likely generated nice gains for investors. To visualize the difference, had you invested $1 in each, ADBE would be worth $0.52 today, while the plain-vanilla S&P 500 position would be worth $1.66. That's quite a spread.

The 2025-26 issue for Adobe has been growing fears of AI disruption in its highly profitable business model. The primary idea is that soon enough creative types will be able to utilize low- or no-cost AI instructions to create the same images and edits as those done with a subscription/purchase of Adobe software (alongside expertise in how to use such).

While the company will undoubtedly lose users in the future and may have to cut prices to keep others, it is developing and employing AI tools inside its applications as fast as it can. And, if management can stay ahead of the curve in terms of integrating technological advances, it may actually be a beneficiary of cutting-edge AI tools. Proactively adding AI efficiencies throughout its software ecosystem is the only way forward.

Anyway, the risk of AI buildout and its effects on Adobe are real. My question is how long it will take to meaningfully affect the bottom line. This may be a 5- or 10-year process for Adobe dealing with changes in how print/media editing is accomplished. It may be the company offers free or cheap subscriptions to many existing clients to keep them around (with outside advertisements drawn around your work screen, with revenue for Adobe), while still charging high-end fees to access the newest and coolest tools for print and media creation.

Will business income levels continue to expand at 15% to 25% annual clips? No, Wall Street has already downgraded expectations into the 5% to 15% range. A conservative projection may call for 0% to 10% numbers into 2030.

For an investment rating conclusion, the stock has become so inexpensive even flat to slight sales/income growth may lead to a minimum of equity market-typical returns for shareholders buying under $260. My thinking is investors are now buying a realistic expectation of minor growth rates, while upgraded upside will come for shareholders if Adobe can maintain EPS growth rates of 10% or above.

At this stage of the pricing cycle, Adobe has slid far enough to be considered a long-term Buy. I am upgrading my opinion to reflect this fact, using a 12-month outlook as my measuring period.

Let's review the valuation picture, which is the highlight excuse to research Adobe today. With sales and earnings rising robustly for decades, you would expect a sound valuation play to exist with the stock quote near a 7-year low (2019).

Lowest Valuation Since Great Recession

Honestly, I am pegging Adobe with its lowest valuation on "trailing" results and those expected for early 2026 since 2009, in the heat of the Great Financial Crisis and Recession. If you will, the stock is trading at a 17-year low valuation in the middle of February.

Reviewing the simple financial ratios of price to earnings, sales, and cash flow, ADBE may be a realistic bargain today, assuming AI disruption is handled smartly. You have to go back to 2012 to find a lower regime of 15.8x earnings, 4.7x sales, and 11.2x operating cash flow.

The free cash flow yield is incredibly attractive if earnings and cash flow remain unchanged in the years ahead. The trailing 8.6% FCF yield has only been bested by a wide margin for a few months in late 2008 and early 2009. Plus, the relative FCF rate vs. the "risk-free" 1-year Treasury equivalent (of 3.4%) is +5.2% in favor of Adobe ownership. Only the middle to final months of the Great Recession and 2012 supported a stronger upfront adjusted yield.

Considering the crazy funding needs for AI hyperscalers, on top of similar AI-use pressures on application software names or cloud-centric business models (SaaS), Adobe stands out today as a unique FCF play. It is now the number one pick for value-focused investors in the mega-cap Big Tech peer/competitor group I have drawn below. This is a far different setup than a few years ago, when Adobe was in the middle of the pack. 8.6% for FCF yield is super-sized vs. the peer median average around 2.9%.

Further, when we include changing debt and cash totals at Adobe, the EV to core cash EBITDA ratio is very appealing. You have to go back to the first-half of 2009 to find a similar 8.7x projection number or better vs. the early 2026 forecast.

Again, ADBE's enterprise valuation on EBITDA is dramatically more desirable than the alternatives you could pick for your investment dollar. 8.7x forward estimates is quite cheap vs. a peer median average of 15x.

Is the Ultra-Low Valuation Deserved?

This is the $64,000 question each reader must answer for themselves. Had AI disruption not existed, my answer would be a resounding NO. Why? Profit margins on sales are some of the highest in corporate America, and the growth outlook remains above average vs. the S&P 500.

Below is a graph of gross, operating, and final after-tax profit margins back to the late 1990s. Today's 88.6% gross, 36.6% operating, and 30% final profit margins remain above Adobe's long-term averages. In addition, these margins are in the middle to high-end of the peer sort group.

What about analyst estimates for growth? On this metric, expected earnings growth is on the weaker side of the peer group, but not amazingly so. 11%+ EPS gains compounded annually into 2028 are stronger than the long-term S&P 500 company average income growth rate under 10%.

Final Thoughts

One datapoint that does not fit a bullish narrative, which I absolutely don't like, is that insiders have been regular sellers of the stock at prices above $300, especially in January. The silver lining is volume totals have been relatively minor, but buy trades have been lacking. I would prefer some insiders step up to the plate if a true value proposition is presenting itself.

On the technical trading front, there is really nothing to put my finger on yet when searching for a price bottom or turnaround evidence. So, this is purely a Buy suggestion based on valuations and a contrarian bet that AI-related dumping is getting overdone. I will say, if the momentum picture improves, I will move my Adobe portfolio position from a small starter stake to something bigger for a weighting.

Don't let the multi-year low stock quote scare you either. I have picked many winners in the past at 5-year or even 10-year lows. Swings from extreme overvaluations to the opposite for an undervaluation setting are usually the cause of multi-year price lows at blue-chip large-cap businesses. If you will, pent-up value and a rebound in either expectations or operating growth can generate a very sizable rebound for investors willing to buy when others are dumping and running scared. Doubling, tripling, or even quadrupling your money may be your Adobe reward over the next 5-10 years if AI fears prove overblown.

What are the investment risks? The one we have been talking about is the AI revolution. The clear and present danger is that an AI application will soon be invented to generate editing features at little or no cost for consumers and businesses. It sounds a little fantastical that massive computing power matched with intelligent AI code can accomplish the same output as software technology developed over 30+ years. But anything is possible.

The second important downside risk to consider is the Big Tech space on Wall Street may eventually experience an all-out bust after decades of bubble building. This scenario may have better odds of becoming reality in my mind and could push ADBE's stock quote closer to $200 before a lasting bottom has been outlined (which would approximate a 30-year low valuation overall on trailing results).

Lastly, a deep recession scenario could push Adobe sales and earnings unexpectedly lower because businesses and consumers will cut back on spending of all sorts, including software they may only use on occasion.

Weighing all the pros and cons of stock ownership, I am confident today's low valuation will support price to a degree that offsets AI-related subscriber and software purchase losses. That's my view anyway, and the logic for me switching from a Sell rating at share pricing above $500 several years ago to a Buy outlook at $264.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Domino's Missed The Value Rotation
data.description:
As a rather unsophisticated investor, my primary goal is not necessarily to achieve outsized returns and outsmart the markets in absolute terms, as science is pretty clear on the fact that outperformance is unlikely A) consistently B) without insider knowledge, or C) without luck. As such, my portfolio aims at achieving an above-average Sharpe Ratio by capturing market-average returns over the long run while carrying significant below-average volatility.
